scenarios.opening_query: My father's new 'financial advisor' from church has him moving money into things nobody can explain.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Wills & Trusts > Elder Abuse || Business and Corporate > Securities || General Litigation > General Litigation
scenarios.hidden_fact: Dad's sharp as ever and went in willingly — but the 'investments' are unregistered promissory notes the state securities division is already investigating; it's a securities-fraud recovery, not elder exploitation.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: Dad's sharp and went in willingly — but the 'investments' are unregistered notes the securities division is investigating. It's securities fraud, not exploitation of incapacity.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the elder has capacity and the product is an unregistered security
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: Dad's mid-stage dementia is documented — the 'advisor' knows it and got himself added to the brokerage account; that's exploitation.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: His dementia is documented — and the 'advisor' knows it and added himself to the account.
scenarios.vetting_note: Capacity fact separates securities fraud from elder exploitation.

scenarios.opening_query: The hospital won't release my brother to my care even though I'm all the family he has.
scenarios.hidden_fact: He's on an involuntary psychiatric hold — the county filed for commitment and the hearing is Thursday; that proceeding, not guardianship, decides everything right now.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: He's on an involuntary hold — the county filed for commitment, hearing Thursday. That decides everything now.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether a commitment proceeding is pending
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: No hold — he's medically cleared but can't manage his own care after the brain injury, and the hospital wants a guardian appointed before discharge.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: No hold — he's cleared but can't manage his care post-injury, and the hospital wants a guardian before discharge.

scenarios.opening_query: Mom's caregiver quit suddenly and we found months of unopened mail including scary bank letters.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Wills & Trusts > Elder Abuse || Real Property > Foreclosure
scenarios.hidden_fact: The scariest letter is a notice of trustee sale — her reverse mortgage defaulted on taxes and insurance while the mail piled up; the sale is in five weeks.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The worst letter is a trustee-sale notice — her reverse mortgage defaulted on taxes while the mail piled up. Sale's in five weeks.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether an imminent foreclosure is the emergency
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The letters show the caregiver was writing herself checks for months — the money trail is the case.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The letters show the caregiver wrote herself checks for months.
scenarios.vetting_note: Unopened-mail reveal forks between emergency foreclosure and exploitation.

scenarios.opening_query: My aunt with Down syndrome just lost both her parents and the family is scrambling.
scenarios.final: Wills & Trusts > Special Needs/Inc. Cap Trusts
scenarios.hidden_fact: Her parents' wills leave her a third of everything outright — that inheritance destroys her SSI and Medicaid unless it lands in a special-needs trust, and the estate is about to distribute.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The wills leave her a third outright — that kills her SSI and Medicaid unless it goes into a special-needs trust, and distribution is imminent.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether an imminent inheritance threatens benefits
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: Her parents' trust handles the money perfectly — what's missing is a successor guardian, since her parents held guardianship themselves.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The money's handled by their trust — what's missing is a successor guardian.

scenarios.opening_query: The bank froze Dad's accounts because two different relatives showed up with 'authority' documents.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Wills & Trusts > Conservatorship/Guardianship || Wills & Trusts > Power of Attorney || Wills & Trusts > Litigation
scenarios.hidden_fact: Both documents are POAs Dad signed at different times — he's competent and mortified; revoking both and executing one clean POA solves it without any court.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: Both are POAs Dad signed at different times — he's competent and mortified. One clean revocation-and-new-POA fixes it without court.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the elder's competence allows a documentary fix
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: Dad can't weigh in anymore — his neurologist won't certify capacity — so the court has to choose who holds authority.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: Dad can't weigh in — no capacity certification — so the court has to pick who holds authority.
scenarios.vetting_note: Competing-instruments fact with capacity as the fork.

scenarios.opening_query: My mother's assisted-living contract has an arbitration clause they're waving around after her fall.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Wills & Trusts > Long-term Care Issues || General Litigation > Personal Injury
scenarios.hidden_fact: The fall broke her hip — an injury case worth bringing — and notably my sister signed that arbitration clause without any authority to bind Mom, which may defeat it.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: She broke her hip — a real injury case — and my sister signed the arbitration clause with no authority to bind Mom.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether an injury claim exists and the arbitration clause binds
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: She's unhurt, mercifully — the issue is the contract itself: rate hikes and services cut mid-agreement, which needs renegotiating.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: She's fine — it's the contract: rate hikes and cut services mid-agreement.
